participants.name: A (rn0bog)
participants.professional_persona: A self-taught AI practitioner from a polytechnic background, now building recommendation engines for a retail analytics startup. He leverages online courses and hackathons to stay current, and mentors other non-degree holders entering AI.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Strong practical knowledge of collaborative filtering, graph neural networks for customer segmentation, and A/B testing of AI features. Also experienced in deploying models via serverless functions.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Hopes to become a principal ML engineer in retail and launch a community bootcamp for underrepresented groups in AI.

participants.name: Dr. (dsybyw)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Lim is a tenured associate professor at NUS, specializing in reinforcement learning for supply chain optimization. She has led multiple 100 Experiments projects pairing her lab with logistics SMEs.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in reinforcement learning, multi-agent systems, and applied optimization for logistics and transportation. Published in top-tier AI conferences (NeurIPS, ICML).
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to translate her research into widely adopted open-source libraries that help Singapore enterprises automate decision-making. Seeks to mentor young AI talents through company-led training programmes.

participants.name: A (y63qy5)

participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-y63qy5a4
participants.professional_persona: A founder of a health-tech startup that uses AI to detect early signs of chronic diseases from wearable data. I am passionate about making preventive healthcare accessible to all Singaporeans, regardless of income.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to scale my solution to reach 100,000 users in the next three years through government partnerships. Ultimately, I hope to contribute to Singapore's goal of becoming a global leader in AI for health.

participants.professional_persona: A mid-level manager at a logistics SME, I recently led the adoption of an AI route-optimisation tool that reduced fuel costs by 20%. I now advocate for making such technology accessible to other SMEs.
participants.skills_and_expertise: I have strong operational knowledge of supply chain management and project implementation. Through the SMEs Go Digital programme, I learned to evaluate AI tools and train my team to use them effectively.

participants.name: Ravi (j9h6dl)
participants.professional_persona: Ravi is a senior AI research scientist at a government research institute, leading a team on privacy-preserving AI for healthcare. He transitioned from industry to focus on public-good applications.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep expertise in differential privacy, federated learning, and secure multi-party computation. Experienced in deploying PETs in real-world health data collaborations.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to shape national data-sharing frameworks so that hospitals and insurers can collaborate without compromising patient privacy. Plans to contribute to international standards on trustworthy AI.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI researcher who has directly benefited from NAIS 2.0's predecessor programmes, I see these inputs as the foundational resources that will drive the 100 Experiments initiative. The S$500 million total funding is explicitly stated in the policy context, so I take that as ground truth. The S$330,000 cap per 100E project is also stated. For compute, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours based on my experience: the government has committed to significant compute infrastructure, and given the scale of 100E (targeting 100 projects), this allocation seems realistic for supporting experimentation without over-provisioning. For trainers, I estimate 50 personnel, drawing from my knowledge of AI Singapore's AIAP programme and the need for hands-on mentorship; this is a modest but credible number given the specialized expertise required.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so I use that as the total funding figure.
The policy context also states 'co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so I adopt that as the maximum co-funding per project.

Based on my experience with compute-intensive RL experiments, 500,000 GPU hours is a reasonable allocation for 100 projects, assuming each project gets roughly 5,000 hours for development and testing.

From my involvement in AI Singapore's programmes, I estimate 50 trainers are needed to effectively guide enterprises and practitioners, given the specialized nature of AI and the need for one-on-one mentoring.

initial_posts.narrative: As a health-tech startup founder, I see the 100E programme as a direct pathway to solve real-world problems with AI. With S$500M total funding and 50 trainers available, I estimate 20 enterprise outreach sessions to engage companies across sectors like healthcare, logistics, and finance. This is based on my experience that meaningful outreach requires multiple touchpoints per sector. For matchmaking, I estimate 10 events to pair researchers with enterprise challenges, as each event can handle 10-15 pairings, and we need to cover diverse problem statements. Training workshops: 15 seems right given 50 trainers can run multiple parallel sessions, and each workshop can train 20-30 participants. Platform onboarding: 100 assists, one per approved project, as the 100E target is 100 projects. My startup's own experience with AI deployment confirms that hands-on onboarding is critical for adoption.

100 platform onboarding assists: One assist per approved project is standard, as my startup's experience shows that dedicated onboarding ensures successful AI experimentation and reduces failure rates.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group reached strong consensus on all four impact variables, estimating Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 5, AI sector GDP share at 3.8%, public AI trust at 65%, and AI talent retention at 70%. Key causal mechanisms included the 100E programme's deployment of 70-75 projects, a growing AI practitioner pool of 11,000-12,000, and 80-85 generative AI use cases, which together drive improved global standing and economic contribution. Public trust is seen as cautiously optimistic, tempered by concerns over data privacy and algorithmic bias, especially in healthcare. Talent retention is supported by a growing local ecosystem but challenged by competition from higher salaries abroad. Minor variations in deployment counts (70 vs 75) did not affect the final estimates, reflecting a shared view of the programme's impact.
